channel.title: zomGreg
channel.description: A loaded demi-god complex, cock it and pull it.

item.title: Your cloud strategy is bad and you should feel...like you have company.
item.description:
I said you have company. Not good company, just company. Plenty of company.

I've been working with/on/around enterprise cloud initiatives for 5 years, and during that time I've had the privilege of closely connecting with about 15 serious attempts at true transformative cloud adoption. I count one as a success. One.

It's logical to conclude that if I'm the common thread on these projects and my "track record" is a terrible 7%, then my leadership/contributions could be the source of the problem. I'm going to assume that's not the case and try to highlight my perception on what prevented success.

Emergent Technology

Cloud is new. Yes, it's still new. I know we're already on to Docker and whatever else will disrupt cloud, but cloud is still new, still disruptive to the enterprise, and medium to large sized corporations are still trying to figure it out. They're trying to figure out what this means to their processes, work flows, customers, resources, and personnel.

Cloud is new and 14/15 enterprise organizations don't have the internal fortitude to weather the disruption.

Virtualization = Cloud

This is a category error. Organizations that are just breathing a sigh of relief that they've "got that whole physical-to-virtual thing" handled are often imbued (read: encumbered) with the notion that cloud is just a fancy version of virtualization. This is equivalent to concluding that because humans have mastered the technology of the wheel, they've got the whole transportation thing figured out.

Virtualization is necessary, but not sufficient, for cloud.

Enterprise Paradigm

Organizations that paid mountains of technical debt to transform their technological ecosystem to a virtualized one are again empowered (read: encumbered) with a team of virtualization experts. These virtualization experts have built careers undertanding and delivering virtual resources to their user base.

Worse, the virtualization team is being tasked with what is the equivalent of technological alchemy. The executive leadership is asking their virtualization experts to turn virtualiztion into cloud. Worse worse, vendors (not mentioning names, but it rhymes with "see em scare") are all too eager to perpetuate the notion that, at a price, a steep, steep price, your trusted partner in virtualization can help you "cloudify" your virtualized infrastructure.

Cloudification is only half the battle. Even if that were a thing that could be done, an equally daunting challenge of transforming the ecosystem that was built around the virtualized enterprise.

Users

Compounding these challenges are the informed end users. Back when the virtualization revolution was underway, the notion of virtualized resource provisioning was an eventuality that end users didn't really understand, much less expect, until it magically appeard one day. The same is not true of cloud. Cloud consumers are a different breed with an entirely different set of expectations.

The accessibility of publicly available, on-demand, self-provisioning is a game-changing, lifestyle-altering expectation for this generation. They don't see it as a thing they should have to wait for the bureaucratically-minded executives to get around to implementing becuase it's already here.

EC2 is the benchmark, fair or not, for service, speed, and features, that cloud consumers expect. Virtualized enterprise delivery of compute resources through complex procurement channels looks like a horse-and-buggy to a Tesla Model S driver.

Enterprise doesn't understand this expectation, and worse, enterprise seeks to control cloud use through time-tested risk management processes. But this will only work for so long, because the users know what's possible, and nothing will stop them from finding a way to take control of their compute future.

Thoughts

The single success case I identified had these characteristics:

  1. They expect cloud adoption to be disruptive. Disruptive to process, policies, workflows, etc. But they stayed the course. They evolved.
  2. End users were empowered, included, and involved in the transformation process. Working groups, internal webinars, and other knowledge-sharing events helped the entire organization view the transformation as a journey they were taking together.
  3. They set a 2-4 year roadmap for full transformation. This was unique to this group because many other enterprise teams expected a very fast transformation process of only a few months.
  4. Mature, cloudy enterprise will take time. The disruption should be positive. End users should feel empowered by the transformation, managers should feel informed and in control.
item.dc:creator: Greg Moselle

item.title: Cloud Automation - General Form
item.description:
Automate application deployment using the cloud! Sure, let's do that, right after we figure out if the application fits a cloud deployment model and see how well the application is known.

The goal when planning for application automation in the cloud is to be able to push the "go button" and have the application stack be completely deployed without the involvement of a human being.

Additionally, should a failure occur, the application stack should be able to be restored to full strength without human intervention.

A tall order, no doubt. How to get there from here:

Getting Started

The first step in planning for deployment of a cloud application is to map out the following cases:

  1. What are the steps involved in achieving a running application?

    Starting from building blocks like software build artifacts and a base image, for example, how does the application reach a functional state?

    If part of the steps involves the database team and security teams, include them in this discussion.

    Map the steps out serially as though the goal was to create a tutorial for deploying the application. Don't try to automate yet, it will only impede later steps.

    Done with that part? Don't worry if the steps have to be revised, that's part of the process.

  2. Draw the finished application architecture.

    This is usually the first step taken by application designers, and that's fine. Try not to group things together that are not alike. For example, separate into tiers the web, application, and database elements, as appropriate.

  3. Break it and map the steps to fix the problem.

    Now that the application architecture is created. Pick an essential element such as a application node and walk through the steps that need to happen in order to restore the application stack to full strength if that node breaks.

    I'll shortly argue that there are a general set of steps involved in this process, but this exercise is vital to the task of automating the overall application.

  4. Scale it and map the steps required to accommodate that change.

    Scaling is often another expectation for applications deployed to the cloud. A similar process to handling breakage can be used in determining the steps required to scale the application at each point where scaling is required.

    We'll walk through just such a case next.

Applications - A General Form

Many application architectures can be reasonably approximated by three tiers: a web, application, and database tier.

As with any approximation, there is some loss of granularity, for example I did not include a load balancer in this diagram, as that function can and often is performed at the web tier by software such as nginx or apache.

A very large amount of applications can be approximated by this diagram. Tomcat, many LAMP applications, and others require these elements.

Change: A Scaling Event

Following the steps listed above, let us follow the case of a scaling event that results in the addition of another application node.

If the process to determine the need to scale was automated, the framework/utility in which the application was deployed needs to be able to handle such events.

In any case, once the application node has joined the application tier, what are the steps that need to happen to handle this change?

Generally, scaling should be used to "right size" the pool of resources. Scaling results in the addition of extra nodes to meet demand or the reduction in the number of nodes in response to decreased demand.

In this case, an application node has joined the application tier. Dependent services running in the web and database tier are affected by this change. For example, nodes in the web tier might need to know about this change in order to start directing traffic to the newly christened application node.

The database nodes in the database tier need to know to allow for incoming traffic from the new application node.

The new application node itself also requires information if it is to be a fully functional member of the application stack. It needs to know how to reach and authenticate to the database nodes, and to allow for traffic from the web nodes.

To make things even more rigorous, the configuration steps taken on the web and database tiers should be taken after the configuration of the application node is complete, but more on that later.

The steps to notify the web and database tiers should happen almost simultaneously. The proper ordering of automation events includes the complete configuration but not the starting of the application node's application at this stage.

Once the dependent services are aware of and have made accommodations for the newly joined application node, the cloud automation framework should trigger the starting of the application on the new application node.

Next Steps

In this example we handled a generic scaling case involving the addition of a new node to an application tier.

After presenting the process for automating an example application in this way, the question I most often receive is: That's nice for your application, will it work for mine?

This question is akin to asking if addition will work on adding your numbers like it did on mine.

The answer is, wait for it, it depends. The short answer is it depends on how well you know the application being automated.

To find out whether or not your application can be automated in this way, start with a couple of assumptions:

  1. The cloud automation framework shown in the diagram exists. Hint: it does.
  2. The framework passes information to nodes that need it, when they need it.
  3. The information is passed securely, and is configurable.

Next, ask these questions:

  1. How well do you know the application being automated? This means, among other things, knowing:
    • Where are the configuration files for the application?
    • What are the steps involved in starting up the application?
    • Are there any dependencies for the application such as databases and load balancers?
    • If dependencies exist, how can they be notified of failures or scaling?
  2. What steps need to happen in the event of failure?
  3. What steps need to happen in the event of scaling?

Wrapping Up

Automating infrastructure changes via scaling is relatively trivial. The interesting part comes in the how applications housed upon said infrastructure handle such events.

I hope this post has provided some insight into the consideration required for automating a cloud application.
item.dc:creator: Greg Moselle

item.title: Server Lifetime as SDLC Metric
item.description:
Earlier we looked at gaining insight into the DevOps-ification of an organization.

Let's look at using server "lifespan" as a metric for gauging the cloudiness of a software development life cycle (SDLC) work flow.

Learn to treat servers like code, strive to automate server deploys and you will soon find yourself telling your servers:

I like you, that's why I'm going to kill you first.

Ephemeral Embrace

The ephemeral nature of cloud servers is a concept that seems like it should be straightforward to adopt, but in my experience embracing the ephemerality of a VM is something most organizations/teams are not yet ready to do. Why not? I have a couple of ideas:

  1. They have immature infrastructure management practices.

    Even organizations that have largely virtualized their infrastructure continue to enforce draconian change control and procurement policies. Some managers with whom I've interacted have indicated it takes 9 days from the time a user requests a virtual machine to the time the resource is delivered to the end user. 9. Days. We'd bleed personnel if it took them more than 9 minutes to get a VM.

    If it takes 9 days to obtain a needed resource, as a user I'd sure as hell hold onto that VM for as long as I could.

    The same manager responsible for the 9-day procurement time is also convinced that if users are able to provision their own virtual severs, they would provision ad infinitum. I wonder why...

  2. They have immature SDLC work flows.

    On my team, the build-test-dev cycle is a continuous efficiency loop. To build artifacts from source, deploy, and test should take less than 15 minutes. That's including the provisioning of a cloud server upon which the code is deployed. Want to reproduce a problem? Pull the tag, deploy, and build.

    Many organizations/teams implement this cycle on static server farms, and miss out on leveraging the benefits of using configuration management and ephemeral cloud servers to implement a true end-to-end development cycle.

Measuring Ephemerality

One prominent software company uses server lifespan as a measure of the efficiency of their SDLC processes.

Their target server lifespan is 30 hours. That is, from the time a cloud server is instantiated to the time it is destroyed, no more than 30 hours should have elapsed.

And when the servers are destroyed, that means destroyed, not snapshotted, not imaged, not archived. Really gone.

For how many hours has your development environment been in existence?

The maturity of a cloudy SDLC ensures a repeatable, collaborative, iterative, fail-friendly approach to software development.

Cattle not Pets

Over at Engine Yard there's a good post about cloud servers and how treating them as ephemeral entities can transform software development work flows.

My colleague at blog dot lusis advocates for the "get it working, then tear it down, then get it working again" approach until the process is fully repeatable.

Server builds not working? Shoot the server in the head and do it again until it works.

As an animal lover I'm not the biggest fan of these analogies, so I'll go with the Buddhism concept of Bhavachakra, the wheel of life. The process of birth and rebirth of existence.

Ephemeral Transformation

As cloudy software practices mature, and as the dev-test process matures, expect users to become more and more self-sufficient and efficient at software delivery.

Users, operations and development alike, will begin to treat servers as temporary entities, deployed repeatably and reliably.

Note the extraordinary increase in monthly server creation volume starting in 2013-03. It corresponds directly to a precipitous decrease in average server lifetime.

The last data point in 2013-11 represents 4705 server launches with an average lifespan of 19.4 hours.

The inflection point starting at 2013-03 represents a mature infrastructure management process. Users can be added to the system programmatically and start provisioning virtual resources in less than one hour.

Additionally, software teams began to fully leverage the flexibility of the cloud and created fast development work flows

Another takeaway message is an insight into server sprawl. The total launch count for the months shown is 67882. The cloud capacity for running servers is significantly less than this number, and due to the mature infrastructure management processes, there is no problem of sprawl. Not shown in this chart is a nearly equal number of server terminations when viewed in a month-over-month basis.

Summary

Targeting a low average server lifespan is critical for some software development processes, primarily the development and test phases.

In other cases, a longer server lifespan can be an indicator of reliability.

Using cloud infrastructure to facilitate the maturation software processes is a perfect use case for the cloud.
item.dc:creator: Greg Moselle

item.title: Cloud Management Value Propositions
item.description:
I was recently challenged to distill the value propositions of a cloud management technology into three success criteria. Consumers of cloud management technology are interested in:

1. Multi-cloud Management. The ability to manage multiple cloud accounts with a single user interface and API.

2. Bring self-provisioning to the end user. Empower the demand-side of cloud resources to provision their own resources quickly and securely.

3. Transparency for managers. Enable managers to gain programmatic insight into cloud use.

Multi-Cloud Management

Multi-cloud management is no small technological success. Bringing the ability to manage disparate cloud technologies under a single user interface is orders of magnitude more empowering that managing several email accounts with a single application. While the user interface typically garners the most attention, I'd argue the API is the most interesting facet of the multi-cloud management platform.

Consider the agility a mutli-cloud API brings to an organization. One customer I'm currently working with is interested in being able to "fail fast". Take that objective for what it's worth, but it's sound engineering practice. A multi-cloud API allows for rapid cross-cloud prototyping without all the overhead of building separate modules for each cloud. The API is where it's possible bring the best of what each cloud has to offer to the end user.

Self Provisioning

Bringing self-provisioning to the end user is where there is the most ground to be gained in winning cloud technology converts. For example, I've experienced lead times for procuring compute resources that extend up to 4 weeks for a virtual server.

I've also heard anecdotal reports of up to 6 weeks for the same. Empowering users to self-provision compute resources such as virtual machines and storage on the order of minutes will leave them feeling like they've seen the face of god.

Transparency

Finally, with all the self-provisioning that accompanies user enablement, a need for transparency will arise. The ability to gain insight into cloud use/adoption is valuable for understanding the costs involved and how consumers are actually using the technology.

While cost might be the most obvious metric for tracking cloud infrastructure use, another metric that may be equally compelling is virtual machine lifetime. Traditionally, infrastructure is provisioned and left to exist for as much time as possible, even if the original reason for the procurement has changed. Furthermore, the longer the length of time for which a piece of infrastructure exists is often a viewed as a positive indicator.

In a cloudy infrastructure environment, the opposite metric can apply. One manager of a virtual infrastructure environment told me the metric she's tracking for VM lifetime is 30 days. Any machine in existence for longer than 30 days is viewed as a candidate for termination because it's more likely that VM is forgotten and lost in the cloud than performing a useful function.

Multi-cloud, self-provisioning, and visibility are three key value propositions for a successful cloud management solution.
item.dc:creator: Greg Moselle

item.title: Cloudy Apps: Part 2
item.description:
In part one we looked at cloud application deployment from a know-thy-app and cloud-independent perspective. In this part, we'll look at scaling and notifications.

Scaling

I saved scaling for last on purpose because although scaling is the sexy thing to do in the cloud, being able to scale properly depends on satisfying the above requirements.

The process for determining the scalability of an application is similar to the one used to determine how well the application is known. Use the application diagram again and step through the addition of a node to the application tier/group, keeping in mind the following questions:

  1. What should trigger that scaling event?
  2. When scaling occurs at the application layer, what components in the application architecture should be notified of that event?
  3. What configuration file changes on dependent services does the addition/subtraction of a node at the application layer necessitate?
  4. What firewall changes need to be made to accommodate the addition of a new node or the subtraction of a terminated node?
  5. What notifications should be sent when scaling occurs?

Repeat this process for every logical grouping of scalable resources.

Scaling Triggers

From an infrastructure perspective, scaling is trivial: start new nodes when they're needed, terminate nodes when they're not.

Scaling should be triggered by a metric that means something to your application. Scaling based on CPU/Memory metrics is likely not the best means to determine the scaling needs of your application for two reasons.

First, high CPU/Memory usage can be caused by operating system processes other than those that are driving your application. Second, although your node thinks it has a CPU, it actually has the hypervisor representation of a CPU, and the metrics presented to the OS are necessarily second-hand.

Dependencies

The next three items are really about handling dependencies. Generically, dependencies are the questions that have to be satisfied when changes occur in one layer of an application architecture.

An example of a dependency is when an application node joins or leaves the application group, the database group should be notified of this change to, for example, execute a grant/revoke routine to grant/revoke access to a node that joins/leaves the application group.

Dependency satisfaction should be facilitated by the cloud orchestration/automation framework being used to deploy and manage the application stack.

The changes that are triggered during dependency handling should be:

  1. Predictable

    The scaling triggers will govern when the dependencies need to be handled.

  2. Idempotent

    The process that satisfies the dependencies should be able to be run many times, producing the same output.

Notifications

Notifications are really about handling the governance aspect of application automation. When changes/scaling occurs in a scaling group or firewall, are those events published to an API endpoint, message queue, email, or SMS?

Getting insight into what has happened as automated events affect your application is critical for being able to create after-action reports, lessons-learned, and process improvement.

Summary

Deploying applications into the cloud doesn't have to suck. With preparation, knowledge of the application, implementation of an automation/orchestration framework, and a notification system, you can surely control all of the aspects necessary for moving applications into the cloud.

Finally, not all applications are destined for the cloud, and that's okay. Although I work in the cloud every day, I'll freely admit that the cloud operating environment has limitations that prohibit some applications from living in a cloud. Use the guidelines here to help identify candidate applications for migration to the cloud.
item.dc:creator: Greg Moselle

item.title: Cloudy Apps: Part 1
item.description:
Moving applications into the cloud is popular: elastic beanstalk, heroku, and others all make it very easy to move applications into the cloud. The drawback to these products is that they serve a very narrow market, or more specifically, a very narrow application segment.

What if your application doesn't fit the oft-referenced Linux, Apache, MySQL, P{ython,HP,erl} (LAMP) architecture? What if your application is deployed using JBOSS, Tomcat, Jetty, or some other framework altogether?

A colleague of mine is quick to point out that the opportunity to migrate a 'traditional' application to the cloud space can be somewhat limited. The limitation most likely comes from the cloud not being a one-cloud-fits-all application deployment space.

Fortunately, there are approaches to deploying in the cloud that meet what I consider to be the requirements for operating an application in the cloud:

  1. Set reasonable expectations.
  2. Know your application.
  3. Hypervisor agnostic, cloud independent.
  4. Scalable

Set Reasonable Expectations

If you think you can "just use Chef" to deploy your application, you're wrong. Here's why: Products like Chef, Puppet, Enstratius, and others are only as smart as you make them. If part of meeting your deadline of T + 1 month for production release is that your team learn Chef, then you're in for an unpleasant surprise.

Saying "we'll just use Chef" to solve all of our cloud application problems is the equivalent of saying "we'll just use a calculator" to pass the Calculus exam. Chef, Puppet, and Enstratius are extraordinarily powerful cloud orchestration/automation frameworks, but they're not without a learning curve.

Take Away

You'll end up paying your technical debt one way or the other, planning for it will help take some of the sting out of that process.

Know Thy Application

This requirement cannot be overstated. Although this requirement seems obvious, it's unfortunately the first place most would-be cloud application developers fail.

Pull out (or create) an application blueprint or architecture diagram and examine it for components or routines that require manual (human) intervention and mark those items as those in need of special attention.

Here's an exercise I use to determine the cloudiness potential of an application: Using the application architecture diagram, pick a node or component and walk through the exercise of what needs to happen to the architecture as a whole when a failure of that node occurs.

If one of the things that happens during that failure event is a PagerDuty alert is sent to the on-call sysadmin so he/she can change all the DNS entries that need to be updated, that's a good indication the application is not yet ready to be deployed to the cloud.

Configuration Files

I was once on site to work though some early cloud initiatives, one of which was the migration of an application running in the data center to a cloud environment. One of my first questions was where are all the configuration files that are used to configure all of the applications and databases comprising the application.

The answer was disheartening from two perspectives. First, the application configuration files were scattered not just between a couple of developers and sysadmins, but in separate departments that required a monthly meeting to schedule a change. This did not bode well for the scaling objective.

Second, I was informed that it was an unreasonable request that the application owner know where every single configuration for the application was held. Smiling? I wasn't. :)

Firewalls

One of the trickiest parts of automating a cloud application can be the creation and updating of firewall rules. This goes for host-level firewalls like iptables but for cloud-based firewalls/security groups that are present in many clouds. If part of the application modification process is to schedule a firewall modification weeks in advance, your application is not cloud ready.

Network security professionals often cringe at the thought of firewalls being manually updated, but that time is here. Audit the plan, audit the result, but most importantly audit the process.

Take Away

Knowing where all of your configuration files live and how they're created and updated is critical whether you're using Chef, Puppet, Enstratius, or any other cloud framework for application deployment.

Hypervisor Agnostic, Cloud Independent

A cloudy application is decoupled from the underlying infrastructure. The application should not rely on Xencenter, ESX/VMWare, KVM, or any other hypervisor technology.

The particular hypervisor in use by a cloud vendor should be of no concern to the cloud application architect.

If your application depends on cloud (or worse) vendor specific technologies down at the cloud layer, this should serve as an indication that at best your application is not able to operate in a multi-cloud environment. This may be okay in the short term, but in the long term, vendor lock-in at this layer will become very problematic as the cloud space matures.

It's already a multi-cloud world, and marrying an application to a particular set of vendor tools is an inflexible approach to cloud application deployment. The application should leverage, not rely entirely on, specific cloud products.

Take Away

Even if your current initiatives don't include a multi-cloud environment, future-proof your processes by accepting that at some point in the future, your application will have to be multi-cloud capable.

Summary (Part 1)

Have a full knowledge of your cloud application, and plan your application deployment for a cloud- and hypervisor-independent solution.
item.dc:creator: Greg Moselle

item.title: SSH Tunnel
item.description:
SSH is quite a bit more powerful than most people, myself included, give it credit. I'm often in a position where I need to be able to view http or https web pages on a remote system that is not accessible on 80 or 443. The solution is to tunnel https traffic over SSH. Here's how it works.

The Problem

I have a web service running on the remote host on port 443, but no access to that system because it only has a private IP address, for one thing, and it has a firewall blocking port 443 traffic between my home host and it.

The remote host does allow SSH traffic on port 22 from the intermediary host.

To tunnel https traffic to my localhost from the remote host, I do this:

On Remote Host

ssh -R *:8888:localhost:443 root@192.168.122.1 -N

This command will set up a listener on the Intermediary Host on port 8888. As shown:

[root@enf ~]# netstat -tnlup | grep ssh
tcp        0      0 0.0.0.0:22                  0.0.0.0:*                   LISTEN  4461/sshd
tcp        0      0 127.0.0.1:8888              0.0.0.0:*                   LISTEN 31448/sshd: root

Any traffic hitting the Intermediary host machine on port 8888 will automatically be forwarded to the Dell Cloud Manager console machine on port 443, which is where the web service is listening.

However, since it's only listening on 127.0.0.1, we cannot yet access the web service from Home.

On Home Host

sudo ssh -L 443:localhost:8888 root@68.999.999.999 -N

This command will set up a listener on localhost, port 443, as shown.

gmoselle@dragonfly:~$ sudo netstat -an | grep 443
tcp4       0      0  127.0.0.1.443          *.*                    LISTEN

You must have root privileges to set up a listener on a "standard" port.

Hosts File

Finally, to complete the tunnel, I make a hosts file entry so I can access the web service.

127.0.0.1 cloud.myremoteconsole.com

Open a browser, and navigate to https://localhost and the remote remote web service should appear. Way cool.
item.dc:creator: Greg Moselle

item.title: Leaving on a Jet Plane
item.description:
I've been traveling quite a bit last year and this year. I grabbed my historical flight information from the Delta website and used a little awk and a little gnuplot to make some graphs.

Here they are:

Testing Caption

2011 saw more international flights than this year (so far). Had some flights to Seoul and Tel Aviv which helped to drive up the average miles/flight number.

2012 has been many trips to the San Fransisco bay area. Since I love Northern California, it's no real tragedy to be headed there.

How did I do this?

To make these graphs, I used a combination of awk and gnuplot. I started with a file that looked like this:

08-Apr-2011 979
10-Apr-2011 979
08-May-2011 936
13-May-2011 6552
13-May-2011 6552
21-May-2011 936
29-May-2011 4222
30-May-2011 2043
15-Jun-2011 5677
15-Jun-2011 1028
26-Jul-2011 1589
31-Jul-2011 1589
04-Oct-2011 1310
08-Oct-2011 957
08-Oct-2011 528
07-Nov-2011 1310
10-Nov-2011 957
10-Nov-2011 528

Where the first column is the date the flight posted to my skymiles account and the second column is the number of actual miles flown, not the MQM or bonus values.

Enter Awk

Using this information, I wrote a small awk script like this:

BEGIN {

  #initialize some variables
  sum=0
}

  # Compute a running total of miles
  {sum+=$2}

  # Print out the date, miles, running total, and running average
  {print $1, $2, sum, sum/NR}

The output looks like this:

08-Apr-2011 979 979 979
10-Apr-2011 979 1958 979
08-May-2011 936 2894 964.667
13-May-2011 6552 9446 2361.5
13-May-2011 6552 15998 3199.6
21-May-2011 936 16934 2822.33
29-May-2011 4222 21156 3022.29
30-May-2011 2043 23199 2899.88
15-Jun-2011 5677 28876 3208.44
15-Jun-2011 1028 29904 2990.4
26-Jul-2011 1589 31493 2863
31-Jul-2011 1589 33082 2756.83
04-Oct-2011 1310 34392 2645.54
08-Oct-2011 957 35349 2524.93
08-Oct-2011 528 35877 2391.8
07-Nov-2011 1310 37187 2324.19
10-Nov-2011 957 38144 2243.76
10-Nov-2011 528 38672 2148.44

I saved that output to a file, and turned to gnuplot.

Now GNUplot

An example of the gnuplot script for the 2011 image is here:

#!/opt/local/bin/gnuplot

set xdata time
set timefmt "%d-%b-%Y"
set xlabel "Date"
set format x "%m-%Y"

set ylabel "Total Miles"
set y2range [0:7000]
set y2label "Average Miles/Flight \n Individual Flights"
set y2tic 0,1000

set title "2011 Flight Statistics"
set key bmargin

plot "summary.dat" using 1:3 title "Total Miles" lt 3 lw 2 smooth bezier, \
"summary.dat" using 1:4 lt 2 lw 2 smooth bezier title "Average miles/flight" axes x1y2, \
"summary.dat" using 1:2 title "Flights" with impulses lt 4 lw 2 axes x1y2

I smoothed out the running total and the average miles/flight lines to make them look prettier.
item.dc:creator: Greg Moselle
